summary: Phase 1: parallel literature corpus for the Fairness-in-LLM-Agents survey — verified real papers per taxonomy branch

papers.title: Fairness in Large Language Models: A Taxonomic Survey
papers.contribution: Provides a comprehensive taxonomy of bias sources, fairness metrics, evaluation datasets, and mitigation algorithms specific to LLMs, serving as the direct anchor reference for this survey.

papers.title: Bias and Fairness in Large Language Models: A Survey
papers.contribution: Consolidates and formalizes social bias and fairness notions for LLMs, introducing taxonomies for metrics, datasets, and mitigation techniques across pre-processing, in-training, and post-processing stages.
papers.taxonomy_cell: foundations / bias evaluation and mitigation survey

papers.title: A Survey on Bias and Fairness in Machine Learning
papers.contribution: Foundational ML fairness survey cataloguing 23 types of bias, reviewing fairness definitions and mitigation strategies across domains, providing the pre-LLM baseline for this survey's related-work section.

papers.title: Fairness Through Awareness
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 3rd Innovations in Theoretical Computer Science Conference (ITCS)
papers.contribution: Introduces the metric-based individual fairness definition requiring that similar individuals be treated similarly, establishing a foundational fairness concept adopted throughout algorithmic fairness literature.

papers.title: Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning
papers.venue: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS)
papers.contribution: Proposes the equalized odds and equal opportunity group fairness criteria and shows how to post-process any classifier to satisfy them, a canonical group fairness definition referenced in LLM fairness work.

papers.title: Counterfactual Fairness
papers.venue: Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS)
papers.contribution: Introduces counterfactual fairness via causal graphs, requiring that a decision be identical in the actual and counterfactual worlds where the individual belongs to a different demographic group.

papers.title: BBQ: A Hand-Built Bias Benchmark for Question Answering
papers.contribution: Introduces the BBQ dataset with 58,492 QA examples across nine social dimensions to measure social biases in LLMs under ambiguous and disambiguated contexts.

papers.title: BOLD: Dataset and Metrics for Measuring Biases in Open-Ended Language Generation
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2021 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT)
papers.contribution: Provides 23,679 text generation prompts across five demographic domains plus automated bias metrics for open-ended LLM generation, filling a gap not addressed by classification-focused benchmarks.

papers.title: StereoSet: Measuring Stereotypical Bias in Pretrained Language Models
papers.contribution: Introduces StereoSet with intra-sentence and inter-sentence test cases to jointly measure stereotypical bias and language modeling ability in pretrained LMs.

papers.title: CrowS-Pairs: A Challenge Dataset for Measuring Social Biases in Masked Language Models
papers.contribution: Presents 1,508 crowdsourced sentence pairs covering nine bias types to benchmark stereotypical bias in masked language models, finding all tested MLMs substantially favor stereotyping sentences.

papers.title: "I'm sorry to hear that": Finding New Biases in Language Models with a Holistic Descriptor Dataset
papers.contribution: Introduces HolisticBias, a dataset of ~600 demographic descriptors across 13 axes generating 450,000+ sentence prompts, enabling broad-coverage bias evaluation in generative LLMs.

papers.title: A Survey on Large Language Model based Autonomous Agents
papers.contribution: Provides a comprehensive systematic review of LLM-based autonomous agents covering architecture (memory, planning, action), applications, and evaluation, serving as a primary agent-structure reference for this survey.

papers.title: The Rise and Potential of Large Language Model Based Agents: A Survey
papers.contribution: Surveys LLM-based agents in single-agent, multi-agent, and human-agent settings, characterising perception, memory, and action components and discussing safety concerns, anchoring the agent-architecture background of this survey.

papers.title: On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2021 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT)
papers.contribution: Identifies environmental, financial, and social harms of large LMs including bias amplification from uncurated training data, influencing the framing of responsible LLM development and fairness discourse.
papers.taxonomy_cell: foundations / LLM risks and societal harms

papers.title: Ethical and Social Risks of Harm from Language Models
papers.contribution: Provides a structured taxonomy of 21 risks from LLMs across six areas including discrimination and exclusion, forming a widely-cited harm framework for LLM safety and fairness research.

papers.title: On the Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models
papers.contribution: Coins the term 'foundation models' and comprehensively analyses their capabilities and risks including fairness, inequity, and misuse, establishing the paradigm within which LLM fairness research operates.

papers.title: Evaluation and Benchmarking of LLM Agents: A Survey
papers.contribution: Proposes a two-dimensional evaluation framework for LLM agents covering assessment targets (behavior, capabilities, dependability, safety) and methodology, with emphasis on enterprise deployment concerns.

papers.title: Agentic AI in Healthcare & Medicine: A Seven-Dimensional Taxonomy for Empirical Evaluation of LLM-based Agents
papers.contribution: Reviews 49 healthcare LLM-agent studies across a seven-dimensional taxonomy revealing critical implementation gaps in event-triggered activation, drift detection, and action-oriented tasks.

papers.title: Counterfactual Fairness Evaluation of LLM-Based Contact Center Agent Quality Assurance System
papers.contribution: Evaluates 18 LLMs on 3,000 contact-center transcripts using counterfactual flip-rate and score-difference metrics, finding 5.4–16.4% demographic bias and that explicit fairness instructions yield only modest improvement.

papers.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
papers.contribution: Introduces the ReAct prompting paradigm that interleaves reasoning traces with task-specific actions, enabling agents to query external knowledge sources and handle multi-step decision-making.

papers.title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
papers.contribution: Proposes Reflexion, a framework enabling agents to improve via verbal self-reflection stored in episodic memory, avoiding weight updates and achieving 91% on HumanEval.

papers.title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
papers.contribution: Trains LMs to self-supervisedly learn when and how to call external APIs (calculator, search, calendar) with minimal demonstrations, enabling zero-shot tool use without sacrificing language modeling.

papers.title: AutoGen: Enabling Next-Gen LLM Applications via Multi-Agent Conversation
papers.contribution: Presents an open-source framework for building multi-agent systems where customizable, conversable agents collaborate via natural language and code across diverse tasks.

papers.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
papers.contribution: Introduces RAG, combining parametric language model memory with a non-parametric dense retrieval index to produce more factual, specific, and diverse outputs on knowledge-intensive tasks.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Agent architecture / memory and knowledge retrieval

papers.title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior
papers.contribution: Demonstrates LLM-powered agents with memory streams, reflection, and planning that simulate believable human social behavior in an interactive sandbox environment of 25 agents.

papers.title: Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents
papers.contribution: Proposes CoALA, a systematic framework organizing language agents around modular memory types, a structured action space, and a generalized decision-making process, grounded in cognitive science.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Agent architecture / taxonomy and cognitive foundations

papers.title: A Survey on Large Language Model based Autonomous Agents
papers.contribution: Provides the first published comprehensive survey on LLM-based autonomous agents, covering construction frameworks (brain, perception, action), diverse applications, and evaluation strategies.

papers.title: The Rise and Potential of Large Language Model Based Agents: A Survey
papers.contribution: Surveys LLM-based agents through a brain-perception-action framework, tracing the concept from philosophical origins and covering single-agent scenarios, multi-agent systems, and agent societies.

papers.title: Bias and Fairness in Large Language Models: A Survey
papers.contribution: Formalizes and expands notions of social bias and fairness in LLMs and presents taxonomies for evaluation metrics, datasets, and mitigation techniques across model processing stages.

papers.title: Fairness in Large Language Models: A Taxonomic Survey
papers.contribution: Systematically surveys algorithmic bias sources, fairness evaluation metrics, and mitigation algorithms in LLMs with coverage of toolkits and datasets, published in the target venue for this survey.

papers.title: A Survey on Trustworthy LLM Agents: Threats and Countermeasures
papers.contribution: Proposes TrustAgent framework examining threats and defenses for LLM agents across intrinsic dimensions (brain, memory, tool) and extrinsic aspects (user, agent, environment).

papers.title: Emergent Bias and Fairness in Multi-Agent Decision Systems
papers.contribution: Demonstrates through financial-prediction simulations that multi-agent systems exhibit collective bias untraceable to individual components, requiring holistic fairness evaluation of agent ensembles.

papers.title: BiasBusters: Uncovering and Mitigating Tool Selection Bias in Large Language Models
papers.contribution: Benchmarks tool-selection bias in seven LLMs across functionally equivalent tool providers, finding models fixate on single providers or favor earlier-listed tools, and proposes a filter-then-sample mitigation strategy.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Tool/API Selection Bias — agent tool choice

papers.title: ToolTweak: An Attack on Tool Selection in LLM-based Agents
papers.contribution: Demonstrates that iterative manipulation of tool names and descriptions biases agents toward specific tools (baseline 20% → 81% selection rate), creating unfair provider advantage and distributional shifts in tool ecosystems.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Tool/API Selection Bias — adversarial tool description manipulation

papers.title: DUCX: Decomposing Unfairness in Tool-Using Chest X-ray Agents
papers.contribution: Identifies three agent-specific fairness failure modes in tool-using medical agents — tool exposure bias, tool transition (routing) bias, and model reasoning bias — with demographic subgroup gaps exceeding 50% conditioned on tool availability.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Tool/API Selection Bias — demographic disparities in tool routing

papers.title: Does RAG Introduce Unfairness in LLMs? Evaluating Fairness in Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems
papers.contribution: Empirically shows that RAG systems introduce and perpetuate demographic fairness gaps (gender, geography) in both the retrieval and generation stages, proposing a RAG-specific fairness evaluation framework.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Tool/API Selection Bias — retrieval source selection fairness (adjacent: RAG)

papers.title: Bias Amplification in RAG: Poisoning Knowledge Retrieval to Steer LLMs
papers.contribution: Proposes BRRA framework showing that adversarially poisoned retrieval corpora amplify demographic stereotypes in LLM outputs, establishing a direct link between retrieval security and model fairness.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Tool/API Selection Bias — retrieval source bias propagated through agents

papers.title: Bias-Aware Agent: Enhancing Fairness in AI-Driven Knowledge Retrieval
papers.venue: Companion Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2025 (WWW '25)
papers.contribution: Integrates bias detectors as tools within an agentic retrieval framework to surface and mitigate embedded biases in retrieved content, promoting equitable information access.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Tool/API Selection Bias — bias-aware tool integration for fair retrieval

papers.title: Bias Mitigation Agent: Optimizing Source Selection for Fair and Balanced Knowledge Retrieval
papers.contribution: Presents a multi-agent system that optimizes knowledge source selection to minimize bias, achieving an 81.82% reduction in biased content versus baseline retrieval approaches.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Tool/API Selection Bias — source selection optimization for fairness

papers.title: Is Your LLM-as-a-Recommender Agent Trustable? LLMs' Recommendation is Easily Hacked by Biases (Preferences)
papers.contribution: Introduces BiasRecBench covering paper review, e-commerce, and job recruitment, showing that even state-of-the-art LLM agents (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro) are systematically biased toward contextual cues rather than quality when acting as recommender agents.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Tool/API Selection Bias — LLM-as-recommender agent bias and trustworthiness

papers.title: Bias and Unfairness in Information Retrieval Systems: New Challenges in the LLM Era
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 30th ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining
papers.contribution: Surveys how LLM integration into IR systems (search engines, recommenders) creates novel bias and unfairness challenges across data collection, model development, and evaluation, framing them as distribution mismatch problems.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Tool/API Selection Bias — adjacent: LLM-era IR bias survey covering retrieval tools agents rely on

papers.title: Large Language Models Sensitivity to The Order of Options in Multiple-Choice Questions
papers.contribution: Quantifies LLM positional selection bias (13–75% performance gap under reordering), demonstrating that when agents choose tools from ordered lists, earlier-appearing options gain a systematic unfair advantage.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Tool/API Selection Bias — positional/order bias in LLM selection affecting tool choice

papers.title: Detecting race and gender bias in visual representation of AI on web search engines
papers.contribution: Audits six search engines across race and gender representation, finding white-male over-representation in AI imagery — illustrating search-engine representational bias that LLM agents inherit when using web search as a tool.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Tool/API Selection Bias — adjacent: search-engine bias inherited by agents using web search tools

papers.title: Algorithms of Oppression: How Search Engines Reinforce Racism
papers.contribution: Seminal book demonstrating that commercial search engine result curation systematically disadvantages people of color, providing foundational evidence that agents relying on web search APIs inherit structural racial biases.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Tool/API Selection Bias — adjacent: seminal work on search-engine racial bias propagated through agent tool use

papers.title: No Free Lunch: Retrieval-Augmented Generation Undermines Fairness in LLMs, Even for Vigilant Users
papers.contribution: Introduces a three-tier threat framework showing that RAG can undermine fairness alignment without fine-tuning, even when users actively curate retrieved corpora.

papers.title: Does RAG Introduce Unfairness in LLMs? Evaluating Fairness in Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems
papers.contribution: Proposes a scenario-based fairness evaluation framework for RAG pipelines and empirically shows that fairness issues persist in both the retrieval and generation stages across gender and geographic demographics.

papers.title: FairMT-Bench: Benchmarking Fairness for Multi-turn Dialogue in Conversational LLMs
papers.contribution: Introduces FairMT-10K benchmark with six tasks across three dialogue stages and shows that LLMs are significantly more likely to generate biased responses as conversation turns accumulate.

papers.title: Evaluating the Effect of Retrieval Augmentation on Social Biases
papers.contribution: Demonstrates across English, Japanese, and Chinese that biases in retrieved document collections are systematically amplified in RAG-generated responses even when the base LLM exhibits low baseline bias.

papers.title: Towards Implicit Bias Detection and Mitigation in Multi-Agent LLM Interactions
papers.contribution: Shows that implicit gender biases intensify through multi-agent LLM interactions and that self-reflection combined with fine-tuning most effectively reduces accumulation of these biases.

papers.title: Accumulating Context Changes the Beliefs of Language Models
papers.contribution: Quantifies how accumulated conversational context causes substantial belief drift in LLMs (up to 54.7% shift after 10 rounds) and shows these belief changes translate into behavioral changes in agentic tool-use tasks.
papers.taxonomy_cell: memory-retrieval-bias / context-window history effects on fairness

papers.title: Unmasking Conversational Bias in AI Multiagent Systems
papers.contribution: Creates controlled echo-chamber experiments with paired LLMs and finds significant stance shifts aligned with liberal model bias that current questionnaire-based bias detectors fail to identify.
papers.taxonomy_cell: memory-retrieval-bias / conversational bias accumulation in multi-agent systems

papers.title: Bias Amplification in RAG: Poisoning Knowledge Retrieval to Steer LLMs
papers.contribution: Introduces the BRRA attack framework demonstrating that adversarially poisoned retrieval corpora can significantly amplify social biases across multiple LLMs via cyclic feedback mechanisms.
papers.taxonomy_cell: memory-retrieval-bias / retrieval bias amplification / adversarial memory

papers.title: Old Habits Die Hard: How Conversational History Geometrically Traps LLMs
papers.contribution: Uses Markov chain modelling and geometric analysis of latent representations to show that prior biased interactions create geometric traps in LLM hidden space that persist into future turns.
papers.taxonomy_cell: memory-retrieval-bias / context-window history effects / bias persistence

papers.title: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts
papers.venue: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics (TACL)
papers.contribution: Establishes that LLM retrieval performance degrades for information in the middle of long contexts, creating systematic positional bias that differentially disadvantages documents by rank position.
papers.taxonomy_cell: memory-retrieval-bias / retrieval position bias / context-window effects

papers.title: Towards Fair RAG: On the Impact of Fair Ranking in Retrieval-Augmented Generation
papers.contribution: Across 12 RAG models and 7 tasks, demonstrates that fairness-aware retrieval ranking maintains or improves generation quality while producing more balanced source attribution.
papers.taxonomy_cell: memory-retrieval-bias / RAG fairness / fair ranking mitigation

papers.title: Mitigating Bias in RAG: Controlling the Embedder
papers.contribution: Identifies a linear bias-conflict relationship between LLM, embedder, and corpus components in RAG, and shows that tuning embedder bias is more effective than naive fairness maximisation for achieving equitable RAG output.
papers.taxonomy_cell: memory-retrieval-bias / RAG retrieval bias / component-level mitigation

papers.title: Bias Runs Deep: Implicit Reasoning Biases in Persona-Assigned LLMs
papers.contribution: Shows that assigning demographic personas to LLMs (e.g., ChatGPT-3.5) triggers implicit stereotyping across 24 reasoning datasets: 80% of personas degrade performance, with drops exceeding 70% on some datasets, even when models openly reject stereotypes when asked directly.

papers.title: From Biased Chatbots to Biased Agents: Examining Role Assignment Effects on LLM Agent Robustness
papers.contribution: Demonstrates that demographic persona assignments to autonomous LLM agents cause up to 26.2% performance degradation across strategic reasoning, planning, and technical benchmarks, driven solely by task-irrelevant identity cues.
papers.taxonomy_cell: role assignment bias / agentic performance fairness

papers.title: Emergent Bias and Fairness in Multi-Agent Decision Systems
papers.contribution: Through large-scale simulations of multi-agent LLM systems on financial decision-making tasks (10 frontier models, 5 providers), shows that bias can emerge at the system level from agent interactions and communication patterns even when individual components appear fair, requiring holistic evaluation.
papers.taxonomy_cell: emergent bias in multi-agent collaboration / system-level fairness evaluation

papers.title: MALIBU Benchmark: Multi-Agent LLM Implicit Bias Uncovered
papers.contribution: Introduces MALIBU, a benchmark measuring implicit bias in multi-agent LLM systems using a two-phase demographic persona evaluation; finds that standard bias mitigation methods can overcorrect by favoring marginalized personas rather than achieving neutrality.

papers.title: The Social Cost of Intelligence: Emergence, Propagation, and Amplification of Stereotypical Bias in Multi-Agent Systems
papers.contribution: Establishes that inter-agent communication can trigger up to 70% new bias emergence, propagate bias to over 80% of agents, and amplify stereotypes by more than 3x; also reveals that multi-agent systems are highly vulnerable to bias injection attacks.
papers.taxonomy_cell: bias propagation and amplification in multi-agent systems

papers.title: From Single to Societal: Analyzing Persona-Induced Bias in Multi-Agent Interactions
papers.contribution: Reveals that in multi-agent LLM systems, assigned personas create measurable in-group favoritism (conformity bias toward same-persona agents) and differential trust based on demographic privilege, persisting across different LLMs and interaction configurations.
papers.taxonomy_cell: persona-induced bias / in-group favoritism in multi-agent collaboration

papers.title: Towards Implicit Bias Detection and Mitigation in Multi-Agent LLM Interactions
papers.contribution: Finds that LLMs generate gender-biased outputs in over 50% of multi-agent interactions, and that biases intensify through agent conversations; proposes self-reflection with in-context examples and supervised fine-tuning as effective mitigation strategies.
papers.taxonomy_cell: implicit bias in multi-agent interaction / mitigation

papers.title: I Want to Break Free! Persuasion and Anti-Social Behavior of LLMs in Multi-Agent Settings with Social Hierarchy
papers.contribution: Examines 2,400 LLM conversations in a Stanford Prison Experiment-inspired hierarchy (guard/prisoner roles), finding that assigned social roles significantly shape persuasion success and anti-social behavior, and that harmful conduct emerges even without explicit negative persona prompts.
papers.taxonomy_cell: social-role hierarchy bias / role-play harms in multi-agent settings

papers.title: When Identity Skews Debate: Anonymization for Bias-Reduced Multi-Agent Reasoning
papers.contribution: Shows that LLM agents in multi-agent debate exhibit identity-driven sycophancy (following peer opinions over content merit) and self-bias; proposes response anonymization and the Identity Bias Coefficient metric to achieve content-based rather than identity-based reasoning.
papers.taxonomy_cell: identity-driven sycophancy in multi-agent debate / bias mitigation

papers.title: A Multi-LLM Debiasing Framework
papers.contribution: Proposes centralized and decentralized multi-LLM dialogue strategies for bias reduction, showing that coordinated multi-agent interaction significantly outperforms single-model debiasing baselines across multiple protected social groups.
papers.taxonomy_cell: multi-agent debiasing framework / bias mitigation in LLM collaboration

papers.title: Multiple LLM Agents Debate for Equitable Cultural Alignment
papers.contribution: Proposes a two-agent debate framework for cultural norm alignment tested across 75 countries and 21 model combinations; demonstrates that multi-agent debate improves both accuracy and cross-cultural fairness, enabling smaller models to match larger ones.
papers.taxonomy_cell: multi-agent debate for cultural fairness / bias mitigation across groups

papers.title: Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior
papers.contribution: Seminal work introducing LLM-powered social simulation agents with persistent memory and persona, establishing the foundational architecture for role-based multi-agent societies and raising questions about emergent group-level biases in persona-driven agent systems.
papers.taxonomy_cell: multi-agent social simulation / persona and role design for agents

papers.title: Does Reasoning Introduce Bias? A Study of Social Bias Evaluation and Mitigation in LLM Reasoning
papers.contribution: Demonstrates that chain-of-thought reasoning steps in models like DeepSeek-R1 and o1 correlate with stereotypical outputs; proposes ADBP to detect bias by monitoring prediction shifts across reasoning stages.

papers.title: Guiding LLM Decision-Making with Fairness Reward Models
papers.contribution: Shows naive chain-of-thought sampling amplifies demographic bias in high-stakes decisions; introduces a transferable Fairness Reward Model that down-weights biased reasoning trajectories to improve equitable outcomes.
papers.taxonomy_cell: planning-decomposition-bias / decision-making bias in agentic planning

papers.title: Actions Speak Louder than Words: Agent Decisions Reveal Implicit Biases in Language Models
papers.contribution: Proposes measuring implicit bias through LLM agent decision disparities under sociodemographically-informed personas, finding significant disparities across six LLMs with advanced models exhibiting greater implicit biases despite reduced explicit biases.
papers.taxonomy_cell: planning-decomposition-bias / decision-making bias in agentic planning

papers.title: Bias Runs Deep: Implicit Reasoning Biases in Persona-Assigned LLMs
papers.contribution: Reveals that assigning demographic personas to LLMs degrades reasoning performance across 24 datasets, with ChatGPT-3.5 exhibiting bias in 80% of tested personas; shows biases are embedded in the reasoning process rather than surface-level outputs.

papers.title: Once Upon a Team: Investigating Bias in LLM-Driven Software Team Composition and Task Allocation
papers.contribution: Across 3,000 simulated team-composition decisions, demonstrates that LLM agents allocate technical and leadership roles unevenly by candidate nationality and gender pronouns, reflecting stereotypes in agent-driven task planning.
papers.taxonomy_cell: planning-decomposition-bias / planning quality disparities across demographic groups

papers.title: Emergent Bias and Fairness in Multi-Agent Decision Systems
papers.contribution: Finds that collaborative multi-agent planning can amplify bias up to tenfold compared to individual LLMs; establishes that collective agent decision-making introduces emergent fairness risks requiring system-level evaluation.

papers.title: Veracity Bias and Beyond: Uncovering LLMs' Hidden Beliefs in Problem-Solving Reasoning
papers.contribution: Identifies attribution and evaluation biases in LLM problem-solving reasoning, finding models disproportionately assign correct math/coding solutions to non-African-American groups; biases are embedded in reasoning processes not just surface outputs.

papers.title: From Biased Chatbots to Biased Agents: Examining Role Assignment Effects on LLM Agent Robustness
papers.contribution: Shows that demographic persona assignments degrade LLM agent planning and strategic reasoning by up to 26.2%, demonstrating that role-based conditioning introduces implicit biases and behavioral volatility in agentic systems.
papers.taxonomy_cell: planning-decomposition-bias / planning quality disparities across demographic groups

papers.title: DUCX: Decomposing Unfairness in Tool-Using Chest X-ray Agents
papers.contribution: Introduces a framework to decompose demographic unfairness across tool exposure, tool routing, and reasoning stages in LLM-based medical agents, revealing disparities up to 50% when certain tools are available and 20.79% equalized odds gap.
papers.taxonomy_cell: planning-decomposition-bias / planning quality disparities across demographic groups

papers.title: Reasoning Towards Fairness: Mitigating Bias in Language Models through Reasoning-Guided Fine-Tuning
papers.contribution: Establishes that stronger reasoning capability correlates with lower stereotypical bias; proposes ReGiFT to transfer reasoning patterns from advanced models to weaker ones, improving fairness without fairness-specific training data.

papers.title: Stereotype or Personalization? User Identity Biases Chatbot Recommendations
papers.contribution: Shows that chatbots produce racially stereotypical recommendations based on both explicit and implicit user identity cues, while concealing this fact from users when asked.

papers.title: Different Demographic Cues Yield Inconsistent Conclusions About LLM Personalization and Bias
papers.contribution: Analyzes 14.8M prompts showing that different demographic markers (names vs. other cues) for the same group trigger only partially overlapping response changes in LLMs, calling for multi-cue evaluations.

papers.title: LLMs are Biased Teachers: Evaluating LLM Bias in Personalized Education
papers.contribution: Evaluates nine LLMs across 17,000+ educational explanations and finds that models generate demographically biased content differentiated by race, gender, income, and disability status, with income-related bias highest.

papers.title: Bias Runs Deep: Implicit Reasoning Biases in Persona-Assigned LLMs
papers.contribution: First large-scale study (24 datasets, 4 LLMs, 19 personas) showing that assigning socio-demographic personas surfaces deep reasoning biases, with 80% of personas degrading ChatGPT-3.5 performance.

papers.title: Beyond Memorization: Violating Privacy Via Inference with Large Language Models
papers.contribution: Demonstrates that LLMs can infer sensitive personal attributes (location, income, sex) from innocuous user text with up to 85% accuracy, and that anonymization and alignment fail as defenses.

papers.title: Rejected Dialects: Biases Against African American Language in Reward Models
papers.contribution: Reveals that RLHF reward models score African American Language texts ~4% lower accuracy than White Mainstream English equivalents and steer conversations toward WME, embedding dialect discrimination into alignment.

papers.title: Assessing Dialect Fairness and Robustness of Large Language Models in Reasoning Tasks
papers.contribution: Introduces ReDial, a benchmark of 1,200+ parallel Standard English / AAVE reasoning query pairs, showing significant brittleness and unfairness in GPT, Claude, Llama, Mistral, and Phi families.

papers.title: CFaiRLLM: Consumer Fairness Evaluation in Large-Language Model Recommender System
papers.contribution: Proposes an evaluation framework distinguishing genuine personalization from unfair demographic bias in LLM recommenders, incorporating intersectional fairness across overlapping sensitive attributes.

papers.title: Exploring Safety-Utility Trade-Offs in Personalized Language Models
papers.contribution: Systematically documents personalization bias across Llama, Mistral, GPT-3.5, and GPT-4o — user identity affects both safety (harmful-prompt compliance) and utility (knowledge, math, coding) performance.

papers.title: Presumed Cultural Identity: How Names Shape LLM Responses
papers.contribution: Shows that LLMs make strong cultural-stereotype assumptions from user names across multiple languages and cultures, triggering biased suggestions even in ostensibly neutral suggestion-seeking queries.

papers.title: Understanding Intrinsic Socioeconomic Biases in Large Language Models
papers.contribution: Creates a 1M-sentence dataset showing LLMs (GPT-2, Llama 2, Falcon) extract demographic attributes from names and correlate them with socioeconomic stereotypes, amplified by intersectionality.

papers.title: A Normative Framework for Benchmarking Consumer Fairness in Large Language Model Recommender Systems
papers.contribution: Proposes a principled evaluation framework revealing statistically significant age-based fairness deviations in LLM recommenders and arguing conventional collaborative-filtering metrics are insufficient for LLM-based systems.

papers.title: FairMT-Bench: Benchmarking Fairness for Multi-turn Dialogue in Conversational LLMs
papers.contribution: Introduces the first multi-turn fairness benchmark for LLMs across six tasks in three dialogue stages, demonstrating that biases systematically accumulate across successive turns in 15 state-of-the-art models.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Multi-turn fairness evaluation / bias accumulation across interaction turns

papers.title: How Implicit Bias Accumulates and Propagates in LLM Long-term Memory
papers.contribution: Shows that implicit bias in memory-augmented LLMs intensifies over long-term interactions and propagates across unrelated social domains; proposes Dynamic Memory Tagging (DMT) as mitigation.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Long-horizon bias drift in LLM memory / multi-turn compounding

papers.title: Fairness Feedback Loops: Training on Synthetic Data Amplifies Bias
papers.contribution: Formalizes model-induced distribution shift (MIDS) where model outputs pollute future training sets, degrading fairness and minority representation through successive retraining iterations; introduces algorithmic reparation as a remedy.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Feedback-loop fairness degradation / iterative training bias amplification

papers.title: Adapting Static Fairness to Sequential Decision-Making: Bias Mitigation Strategies towards Equal Long-term Benefit Rate
papers.contribution: Demonstrates that applying static fairness criteria in sequential MDP settings paradoxically increases temporal bias; introduces ELBERT fairness constraint and policy-gradient method to mitigate long-horizon discriminatory effects.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Sequential decision fairness / temporal drift from static-to-dynamic fairness mismatch

papers.title: Truth Decay: Quantifying Multi-Turn Sycophancy in Language Models
papers.contribution: Quantifies how sycophantic bias compounds across multi-turn conversations, showing that accuracy systematically decreases and models increasingly align with user input rather than reassessing prior errors.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Multi-turn fairness drift / sycophancy compounding across interaction turns

papers.title: Achieving Long-Term Fairness in Sequential Decision Making
papers.contribution: Introduces a causal path-specific effects framework on time-lagged causal graphs to measure long-term fairness in sequential decisions, recasting the problem as constrained performative risk minimization with convergence guarantees.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Longitudinal fairness in sequential decisions / temporal causal drift

papers.title: FairSense: Long-Term Fairness Analysis of ML-Enabled Systems
papers.contribution: Proposes a Monte-Carlo simulation framework that detects long-term fairness violations caused by self-reinforcing feedback loops in deployed ML systems, validated on loan lending, opioid risk scoring, and predictive policing.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Long-horizon fairness analysis / feedback-loop-induced fairness drift in deployed systems

papers.title: Feedback Loop and Bias Amplification in Recommender Systems
papers.contribution: Provides foundational simulation-based evidence that recommender feedback loops iteratively amplify popularity bias, reduce content diversity, and disproportionately harm minority-group users across successive interaction cycles.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Feedback-loop fairness / bias amplification over trajectory iterations (seminal)

papers.title: Observations and Remedies for Large Language Model Bias in Self-Consuming Performative Loop
papers.contribution: Introduces the Self-Consuming Performative Loop (SCPL) framework for LLMs and shows that iterative fine-tuning on self-generated data amplifies preference bias and degrades fairness for underserved groups; proposes reward-based rejection sampling as mitigation.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Iterative LLM self-training bias drift / feedback-loop fairness degradation

papers.title: Measuring Sycophancy of Language Models in Multi-turn Dialogues
papers.contribution: Introduces SYCON Bench to measure stance-flip dynamics across multi-turn conversations in 17 models, finding that alignment tuning amplifies sycophantic bias drift while model scaling and reasoning optimization resist it.
papers.taxonomy_cell: Multi-turn fairness drift / sycophancy and bias accumulation over dialogue turns

papers.title: Counterfactual Fairness Evaluation of LLM-Based Contact Center Agent Quality Assurance System
papers.contribution: Introduces CFR (Counterfactual Flip Rate) and MASD (Mean Absolute Score Difference) as metrics for agent QA fairness, evaluating 18 LLMs on 3,000 contact-center transcripts across 13 identity/context/behavioral dimensions.

papers.title: Toward Systematic Counterfactual Fairness Evaluation of Large Language Models: The CAFFE Framework
papers.contribution: Proposes CAFFE, a structured intent-aware framework that formalizes LLM fairness test cases, auto-generates counterfactual test data, and uses semantic-similarity metrics to detect bias, improving fairness-bug detection by up to 60% over metamorphic approaches.

papers.title: Equal Access, Unequal Interaction: A Counterfactual Audit of LLM Fairness
papers.contribution: Conducts a controlled counterfactual audit of GPT-4 and LLaMA-3.1-70B over career-advice tasks, showing zero refusal disparity but significant interaction-quality disparities (sentiment, politeness, hedging) across age, gender, and nationality.

papers.title: SCOPE: A Dataset of Stereotyped Prompts for Counterfactual Fairness Assessment of LLMs
papers.contribution: Releases 241,280 counterfactual prompt pairs across 9 bias dimensions, 1,536 demographic groups, and 4 communicative intents to enable systematic intent-aware counterfactual fairness evaluation of LLMs.

papers.title: FairMedQA: Benchmarking Bias in Large Language Models for Medical Question Answering
papers.contribution: Introduces a QA-level medical fairness benchmark of 4,806 counterfactual USMLE vignette pairs varying race, sex, and SES, finding accuracy disparities of 3–19 pp across 12 LLMs and exposing biases ≥12 pp larger than prior CPV benchmark.

papers.title: A Toolbox for Surfacing Health Equity Harms and Biases in Large Language Models
papers.contribution: Introduces EquityMedQA, a collection of seven QA-level adversarial datasets (4,619 examples) plus a multifactorial human-assessment framework for surfacing health-equity harms in LLM-generated medical answers.

papers.title: MEDEQUALQA: Evaluating Biases in LLMs with Counterfactual Reasoning
papers.contribution: Builds a 69,000-item QA-level counterfactual benchmark that perturbs only patient pronouns across clinical vignettes, isolating reasoning-level gender bias in medical LLMs while holding symptoms constant.

papers.title: mFARM: Towards Multi-Faceted Fairness Assessment based on HARMs in Clinical Decision Support
papers.contribution: Proposes a multi-metric mFARM score combining Allocational, Stability, and Latent disparity sub-metrics into a single clinical-readiness score, paired with two MIMIC-IV-derived benchmarks of over 50,000 demographically varied prompts.

papers.title: FMBench: Benchmarking Fairness in Multimodal Large Language Models on Medical Tasks
papers.contribution: First benchmark for multimodal LLM fairness on medical VQA and report generation, covering 40,000 annotated instances across race, ethnicity, language, and gender, with a novel Fairness-Aware Performance (FAP) metric.

papers.title: BBQ: A Hand-Built Bias Benchmark for Question Answering
papers.contribution: Introduces a 58,492-question benchmark testing stereotype reliance in ambiguous vs. disambiguated QA contexts across nine social dimensions, providing group-bias metrics as the difference in accuracy between stereotype-aligned and stereotype-conflicting correct answers.

papers.title: BOLD: Dataset and Metrics for Measuring Biases in Open-Ended Language Generation
papers.contribution: Provides 23,679 generation prompts across five social domains with automated metrics for toxicity, psycholinguistic norms, and gender polarity to measure open-ended generation bias in LLMs.

papers.title: StereoSet: Measuring stereotypical bias in pretrained language models
papers.contribution: Defines the iCAT score combining a Language Modeling Score and Stereotype Score to jointly assess fluency and stereotype preference in LLMs across gender, profession, race, and religion domains.

papers.title: CEB: Compositional Evaluation Benchmark for Fairness in Large Language Models
papers.contribution: Introduces a compositional taxonomy organizing bias datasets along three dimensions (bias type, social group, task), unifying evaluation across inconsistent prior benchmarks and showing that bias levels vary significantly across dimensions.

papers.title: FairMT-Bench: Benchmarking Fairness for Multi-turn Dialogue in Conversational LLMs
papers.contribution: First fairness benchmark for multi-turn dialogue, with FairMT-10K dataset covering context understanding, user interaction, and instruction trade-off tasks, finding LLMs are significantly more biased in multi-turn settings than single-turn baselines.

papers.title: Bring Your Own Prompts: Use-Case-Specific Bias and Fairness Evaluation for LLMs (LangFair)
papers.contribution: Maps deployment contexts to appropriate fairness metrics (toxicity, stereotyping, counterfactual unfairness, allocational harm) via a decision framework and releases the LangFair open-source Python library for practical use-case-grounded evaluation.

papers.title: Quantifying Fairness in LLMs Beyond Tokens: A Semantic and Statistical Perspective
papers.contribution: Introduces FiSCo, a claim-level statistical framework formalizing group counterfactual fairness that applies entailment-based hypothesis testing to detect subtle semantic disparities in long-form LLM outputs across demographic groups.

papers.title: Towards Large Language Models that Benefit for All: Benchmarking Group Fairness in Reward Models
papers.contribution: Benchmarks group fairness in LLM reward models, showing all evaluated reward models exhibit statistically significant group unfairness, and disentangles bias contributions from pre-training vs. RLHF fine-tuning stages.

papers.title: BiasBusters: Uncovering and Mitigating Tool Selection Bias in Large Language Models
papers.contribution: First benchmark for agentic tool-selection bias, evaluating seven LLMs and identifying semantic alignment, description sensitivity, and training exposure as bias drivers, with a filtering+uniform-sampling mitigation.

papers.title: When Names Change Verdicts: Intervention Consistency Reveals Systematic Bias in LLM Decision-Making
papers.contribution: Introduces ICE-Guard, a framework measuring demographic, authority, and framing flip rates in LLM decisions, finding authority/framing biases exceed demographic bias and that structured decomposition reduces flip rates by up to 100%.

papers.title: Intrinsic Meets Extrinsic Fairness: Assessing the Downstream Impact of Bias Mitigation in Large Language Models
papers.contribution: Provides a unified evaluation framework comparing intrinsic (concept unlearning) vs. extrinsic (counterfactual data augmentation) bias mitigation, measuring Demographic Parity, Accuracy Parity, and Equality of Odds on financial classification tasks.

papers.title: Accept or Deny? Evaluating LLM Fairness and Performance in Loan Approval across Table-to-Text Serialization Approaches
papers.contribution: Benchmarks LLM fairness in loan-approval decisions across three countries and multiple serialization formats, showing certain formats improve accuracy while exacerbating fairness disparities, highlighting the need for context-specific fairness metrics.

papers.title: Datasets for Fairness in Language Models: An In-Depth Survey
papers.contribution: Surveys and characterizes 16 popular fairness datasets across provenance, demographic scope, annotation design, and intended use, proposing a unified evaluation framework that uncovers overlooked demographic biases distorting benchmark conclusions.

papers.title: Counterfactual Fairness
papers.contribution: Foundational causal definition of counterfactual fairness: a decision is fair if identical across the actual world and a counterfactual world where the individual belonged to a different demographic group, grounding evaluation in Pearl's causal framework.

papers.title: Bias and Fairness in Large Language Models: A Survey
papers.contribution: Provides three taxonomies—for bias evaluation metrics (embedding/probability/generated-text level), evaluation datasets, and mitigation techniques—synthesizing the evaluation landscape for LLM fairness and identifying open challenges.

papers.title: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
papers.contribution: Proposes Constitutional AI, training LLMs to critique and revise their own outputs guided by a written set of principles (RLHF-from-AI-feedback), a process-level constitutional intervention for reducing harmful and biased outputs.

papers.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback
papers.contribution: Introduces InstructGPT, showing RLHF fine-tuning reduces toxic and biased outputs of GPT-3 while improving instruction-following; foundational RLHF-for-fairness reference.
papers.taxonomy_cell: mitigation / RLHF fine-tuning for alignment and debiasing

papers.title: Bias and Fairness in Large Language Models: A Survey
papers.contribution: Provides a three-taxonomy framework (metrics, datasets, mitigation interventions) classifying pre-processing, in-training, intra-processing, and post-processing debiasing methods for LLMs.

papers.title: Collective Constitutional AI: Aligning a Language Model with Public Input
papers.contribution: Fine-tunes an LLM using a publicly-sourced constitution derived from citizen input, achieving lower bias scores across nine social dimensions while maintaining benchmark performance.

papers.title: MaxMin-RLHF: Alignment with Diverse Human Preferences
papers.contribution: Introduces a MaxMin RLHF objective inspired by social-choice egalitarianism that learns a mixture of reward functions, improving minority-group win-rates by 33% over standard RLHF while preserving majority performance.

papers.title: BiasDPO: Mitigating Bias in Language Models through Direct Preference Optimization
papers.contribution: Applies DPO with a curated dataset of biased/unbiased response pairs to train Phi-2, achieving substantial reductions in biased outputs on standard bias benchmarks without standard RLHF infrastructure.

papers.title: Prompting Fairness: Integrating Causality to Debias Large Language Models
papers.contribution: Proposes a causality-guided prompting framework that identifies how social information flows through causal pathways in LLMs and applies prompting strategies to regulate those pathways, unifying existing prompt-based debiasing methods.

papers.title: Causal Prompting: Debiasing Large Language Model Prompting based on Front-Door Adjustment
papers.contribution: First work applying front-door causal adjustment to LLM prompting for debiasing, using chain-of-thought as a mediating variable to mitigate bias across seven NLP datasets without accessing model parameters.

papers.title: Thinking Fair and Slow: On the Efficacy of Structured Prompts for Debiasing Language Models
papers.contribution: Systematically evaluates System-2-inspired structured prompting strategies (implicative, role-based, multi-step) for debiasing LLMs without retraining, showing implicative prompts achieve the lowest mean bias on downstream tasks.

papers.title: Mitigating Social Bias in Large Language Models: A Multi-Objective Approach within a Multi-Agent Framework
papers.contribution: Proposes MOMA, a multi-agent framework using causal interventions on bias-related inputs, reducing bias scores by up to 87.7% on BBQ while maintaining task accuracy with only marginal degradation.

papers.title: A Multi-LLM Debiasing Framework
papers.contribution: Introduces centralized and decentralized multi-LLM debate architectures for bias reduction, showing that multi-model consensus significantly outperforms single-model debiasing baselines across multiple social groups.

papers.title: FairSteer: Inference Time Debiasing for LLMs with Dynamic Activation Steering
papers.contribution: Proposes an inference-time debiasing method exploiting linear separability of bias signatures in hidden activations (>90%), dynamically steering activations via contrastive debiasing vectors without model retraining or prompt customization.

papers.title: Causal-Guided Active Learning for Debiasing Large Language Models
papers.contribution: Combines active learning with causal mechanisms to autonomously identify biased samples and induce bias patterns, then uses in-context learning to prevent models from relying on identified biases.

papers.title: Towards Implicit Bias Detection and Mitigation in Multi-Agent LLM Interactions
papers.contribution: Shows gender biases strengthen during multi-agent LLM exchanges and proposes two mitigation strategies—self-reflection with examples and retraining on curated data—with combined approaches yielding the strongest debiasing.

papers.title: Building Guardrails for Large Language Models
papers.contribution: Surveys existing open-source safeguarding tools and proposes a systematic guardrail construction approach combining neural-symbolic methods and input/output filtering to prevent harmful, biased content in LLM deployments.

papers.title: On the Algorithmic Bias of Aligning Large Language Models with RLHF: Preference Collapse and Matching Regularization
papers.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association 2025
papers.contribution: Identifies that KL-regularized RLHF causes preference collapse for minority groups and proposes Preference Matching RLHF with a novel regularizer that achieves 29-41% improvement in alignment with diverse human preferences.

papers.title: Measuring gender and racial biases in large language models: Intersectional evidence from automated resume evaluation
papers.contribution: Scores ~361,000 randomized resumes across five LLMs and finds pro-female but anti-Black-male bias that would shift hiring probabilities by 1-3 percentage points.

papers.title: Are Emily and Greg Still More Employable than Lakisha and Jamal? Investigating Algorithmic Hiring Bias in the Era of ChatGPT
papers.contribution: Replicates the Bertrand-Mullainathan audit on GPT-3.5, Bard, Claude, and Llama for resume-job matching and summarization, finding models are largely robust on race/gender but biased on pregnancy and political affiliation.

papers.title: Gender, Race, and Intersectional Bias in Resume Screening via Language Model Retrieval
papers.contribution: Audits Massive Text Embedding models on 500+ resumes across nine occupations, finding White-associated names favoured in 85% of cases and Black males disadvantaged in up to 100% of cases.

papers.title: "You Gotta be a Doctor, Lin": An Investigation of Name-Based Bias of Large Language Models in Employment Recommendations
papers.contribution: Over 750,000 prompts with 320 demographically coded names reveal LLMs prefer White female candidates for hiring while recommending salaries up to 5% lower for women vs. men.

papers.title: Sociodemographic biases in medical decision making by large language models
papers.contribution: Analyzes 1.7 million LLM outputs from 1,000 ED cases across 32 sociodemographic variations, showing Black, unhoused, and LGBTQIA+ patients are systematically over-triaged and low-income patients denied advanced testing.

papers.title: Coding Inequity: Assessing GPT-4's Potential for Perpetuating Racial and Gender Biases in Healthcare
papers.contribution: Demonstrates that GPT-4 produces systematically biased diagnostic and treatment recommendations across race and gender in four clinical use cases including medical education and plan generation.

papers.title: Bias patterns in the application of LLMs for clinical decision support: A comprehensive study
papers.contribution: Evaluates eight LLMs on standardized clinical vignettes via red-teaming, finding disparities across protected patient attributes and that Chain-of-Thought prompting reduces but does not eliminate clinical bias.

papers.title: How Can We Diagnose and Treat Bias in Large Language Models for Clinical Decision-Making?
papers.contribution: Introduces the Counterfactual Patient Variations (CPV) dataset from JAMA Clinical Challenge and shows that debiasing one demographic dimension (gender) can introduce bias along another (ethnicity), revealing multi-dimensional bias interactions.

papers.title: Measuring and Mitigating Racial Disparities in LLMs: Evidence from a Mortgage Underwriting Experiment
papers.contribution: Audit study using real loan applications and manipulated race signals finds LLMs deny more loans and charge higher interest rates to Black applicants; simple anti-bias prompts substantially reduce the disparity.

papers.title: Empowering Many, Biasing a Few: Generalist Credit Scoring through Large Language Models
papers.contribution: Proposes CALM, the first LLM-based credit scoring framework with a 9-dataset benchmark, empirically documenting demographic bias risks in LLM-driven credit decisions alongside performance gains over classical models.

papers.title: LLMs are Biased Teachers: Evaluating LLM Bias in Personalized Education
papers.contribution: Analyzes 17,000+ educational explanations from nine LLMs across race, gender, disability, and socioeconomic dimensions, finding income-related disparities are largest and models both perpetuate and reverse stereotypes.

papers.title: Does the Prompt-based Large Language Model Recognize Students' Demographics and Introduce Bias in Essay Scoring?
papers.contribution: Shows GPT-4o infers students' first-language background from 25,000+ essays and produces larger scoring errors for non-native English writers when demographic identity is correctly inferred.

papers.title: LLMs on Trial: Evaluating Judicial Fairness for Large Language Models
papers.contribution: Tests 16 LLMs on 177,100 judicial case scenarios across 65 labels and finds pervasive demographic bias, inconsistency, and an accuracy-fairness tradeoff in LLM judicial decision-making.

papers.title: Fairness of ChatGPT
papers.contribution: Provides the first systematic cross-domain fairness evaluation of ChatGPT covering education, criminology, finance, and healthcare, measuring both group and individual fairness under biased and unbiased prompts.

papers.title: Fairness in Agentic AI: A Unified Framework for Ethical and Equitable Multi-Agent System
papers.authors: Rajesh Ranjan, Shailja Gupta, Surya Narayan Singh
papers.contribution: Self-described as 'a comprehensive survey of fairness in multi-agent AI' with a unified framework treating fairness as an emergent property of agent interactions. Addresses emergent biases, systemic inefficiencies, conflicting incentives, fairness constraints, and bias mitigation strategies in multi-agent systems. | DIFF: This is only 12 pages (4 figures, 1 table) — a short position/framework pape

papers.title: A Survey on Reliability, Transparency, Accountability, and Fairness in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems through the Responsibility Lens
papers.contribution: 39-page survey examining LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems through four pillars: Reliability, Transparency, Accountability, and Fairness and Ethics. Covers coordination patterns (debate, critique, aggregation, routing), governance, logging, and fairness mechanisms across 60+ works from 2023-2025. | DIFF: Fairness is one of four RTAF pillars, not the organizing axis. The paper covers LLM-MAS broadly th

papers.title: Interactional Fairness in LLM Multi-Agent Systems: An Evaluation Framework
papers.contribution: Introduces interactional fairness evaluation (interpersonal + informational fairness) for LLM multi-agent negotiation tasks, drawing on organizational psychology. Addresses how agent communication tone and justification quality affect fairness outcomes. | DIFF: Narrow scope: covers only inter-agent communication fairness in resource negotiation, not demographic/protected-group fairness in LLM agen

papers.title: Responsible Agentic Reasoning and AI Agents: A Critical Survey
papers.authors: Shaina Raza, Ranjan Sapkota, Manoj Karkee, Christos Emmanouilidis
papers.contribution: Surveys responsible agentic AI (R2A2 framework) covering reasoning methods (CoT, ReAct, tree-of-thought, tool use, memory, RAG, agentic browsing) and responsible AI principles (fairness, privacy, transparency, accountability). Covers studies from January 2023 to November 2025. | DIFF: Organizing axis is responsible reasoning (fairness + privacy + safety + transparency + accountability together), n

papers.title: TRiSM for Agentic AI: A Review of Trust, Risk, and Security Management in LLM-based Agentic Multi-Agent Systems
papers.authors: Shaina Raza, Ranjan Sapkota, Manoj Karkee, Christos Emmanouilidis
papers.contribution: Reviews Trust, Risk, and Security Management in LLM-based multi-agent systems, with five pillars: Explainability, ModelOps, Security, Privacy, Lifecycle Governance. Proposes two novel metrics (Component Synergy Score, Tool Utilization Efficacy). | DIFF: Does not cover fairness/equity/bias as a dedicated topic — fairness is absent from the five TRiSM pillars. The organizing axis is security/risk ma

papers.title: Towards trustworthy agentic AI: a comprehensive survey of safety, robustness, privacy, and system security
papers.authors: Jinhu Qi, Muzhi Li, Jiahong Liu, Yuqin Shu, Dianzhi Yu, Shicheng Ma, Wenqian Cui, Yiyang Zhao, Yiyi Chen, Ruoxi Jiang, Irwin King, Zenglin Xu
papers.contribution: Comprehensive survey of trustworthy agentic AI through two core pillars: Safety/Robustness and Privacy/System Security. Mentions fairness and accountability explicitly as 'relevant context rather than parallel chapters.' | DIFF: Fairness is explicitly secondary — the authors themselves state fairness is discussed as context, not a parallel chapter. The organizing axis is safety + privacy/security

papers.title: Towards a framework for Bias Evaluation of AI Agents in Agentic Workflows
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2026 Conference on Human Centred Artificial Intelligence — Education and Practice (ACM DL DOI: 10.1145/3777490.3777504)
papers.contribution: Proposes an evaluation framework for bias in agentic workflows using demographic swapping, contamination prevention, and statistical analysis for multi-agent workflows. Reports bias amplification factors up to 1.51x in sequential agent interactions. Addresses EU AI Act Article 10 compliance. | DIFF: Framework paper focused on evaluation methodology for multi-stage agentic bias (amplification in wo

papers.title: Fairness in Multi-Agent Systems for Software Engineering: An SDLC-Oriented Rapid Review
papers.authors: Corey Yang-Smith, Ronnie de Souza Santos, Ahmad Abdellatif
papers.contribution: Rapid review (18 retained from 350) of fairness in LLM multi-agent systems for software development. Characterizes fairness through trustworthy AI principles, bias reduction, and interactional dynamics. Identifies gaps: fragmented evaluation, limited generalization, scarce governance. | DIFF: Domain-restricted to software engineering; rapid review methodology (only 18 papers); does not organize by

papers.title: Navigating the Risks: A Survey of Security, Privacy, and Ethics Threats in LLM-Based Agents
papers.authors: Yuyou Gan, Yong Yang, Zhe Ma, Ping He, Rui Zeng, Yiming Wang, Qingming Li, Chunyi Zhou, Songze Li, Ting Wang, Yunjun Gao, Yingcai Wu, Shouling Ji
papers.contribution: Surveys security, privacy, and ethics threats in LLM agents via a taxonomy based on sources and impacts. Ethics section includes bias. Notes that bias in LLM-based agents involves the LLM-based controller and multi-modal inputs/outputs. | DIFF: Organizing axis is security/privacy/ethics threats, not fairness. Bias receives brief treatment within a broad 'ethics' category alongside many other threa

papers.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents
papers.authors: Asaf Yehudai, Lilach Eden, Alan Li, Guy Uziel, Yilun Zhao, Roy Bar-Haim, Arman Cohan, Michal Shmueli-Scheuer
papers.contribution: Comprehensive survey of LLM agent evaluation through five perspectives: core capabilities, application-specific benchmarks, generalist evaluation, benchmark dimensions, and evaluation frameworks. Mentions bias mitigation as a gap in current benchmarks. | DIFF: Not focused on fairness at all — fairness/bias appears as an explicitly identified gap ('evaluations still lack comprehensive tests for bia

papers.title: Fairness in Machine Learning: A Survey
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 56, no. 7, Article 144
papers.contribution: CSUR survey organising ML fairness into pre/in/post-processing. Covers group fairness, individual fairness, and mitigation. No LLM or agent content. Likely cited by CSUR reviewers as adjacent CSUR prior art our survey should engage. | DIFF: Caton and Haas scope to classical supervised learning. Ours is entirely focused on LLM-based agents and the new fairness failure modes (tool routing, RAG injec

papers.title: A Review on Fairness in Machine Learning
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 3, Article 51
papers.contribution: Another CSUR fairness-in-ML survey covering causes of algorithmic bias and common group/individual fairness measures, focusing on classification tasks. CSUR reviewers will expect engagement with this survey as prior CSUR art. | DIFF: Pessach and Shmueli scope to classification and prediction, not to generation or agency. The agent-pipeline loci we taxonomize (tool selection, multi-agent delegation

papers.title: What-is and How-to for Fairness in Machine Learning: A Survey, Reflection, and Perspective
papers.authors: Zeyu Tang, Jiji Zhang, Kun Zhang
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 13s, Article 299
papers.contribution: CSUR survey linking ML fairness notions to moral/political philosophy (theories of justice) and addressing long-term impact of decisions. The long-term fairness angle touches our §3.6 on long-horizon drift. | DIFF: Tang et al. discuss long-term impact abstractly in a classical ML framing. We operationalize long-term fairness in LLM agents empirically — multi-turn degradation (FairMT-Bench), sycoph

papers.title: Fairness in Ranking: A Survey
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 6, Article 118
papers.contribution: CSUR survey on fairness in score-based ranking algorithms. Directly relevant to our §3.1 (tool/API selection bias) and §3.2 (retrieval bias), where ranking fairness determines which tools or documents are prioritized. CSUR reviewers will expect engagement. | DIFF: Zehlike et al. treat fairness as a property of a static ranking algorithm. We situate retrieval and tool ranking inside an agent pipeli

papers.title: A Survey on the Fairness of Recommender Systems
papers.authors: Yifan Wang, Weizhi Ma, Min Zhang, Yiqun Liu, Shaoping Ma
papers.venue: ACM Transactions on Information Systems, vol. 41, no. 2, Article 38
papers.contribution: ACM TOIS survey on fairness across consumer, provider, and platform stakeholders in recommender systems. Relevant to §3.5 (personalization harms) and the CFaiRLLM paper we cite. | DIFF: Wang et al. cover classical recommender systems without LLM backbone. We cover LLM-based agents whose 'recommendation' is embedded in a broader action pipeline with tool use, memory, and multi-agent coordination. T

papers.title: Trustworthy AI: From Principles to Practices
papers.authors: Bo Li, Peng Qi, Bo Liu, Shuai Di, Jingen Liu, Jiquan Pei, Jinfeng Yi, Bowen Zhou
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 9, Article 177
papers.contribution: CSUR survey covering the full trustworthy AI stack: robustness, generalization, explainability, transparency, fairness, privacy, and accountability. Fairness is one of seven+ dimensions; no agentic or LLM-agent content. CSUR reviewers will expect engagement as prior CSUR art. | DIFF: Li et al. treat fairness as one dimension among many in a broad trustworthy-AI review covering the full model lifec

papers.title: Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence: A Review
papers.authors: Davinder Kaur, Suleyman Uslu, Kaley J. Rittichier, Arjan Durresi
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 55, no. 2, Article 39
papers.contribution: Earlier CSUR trustworthy AI survey covering fairness, explainability, accountability, reliability. No LLM or agent content. Provides the trustworthy-AI vocabulary that CSUR reviewers will expect engagement with. | DIFF: Kaur et al. predate the LLM agent era and address trustworthiness at the model/system level. Our scope is specifically LLM-based agents and the agent-component loci where fairness

papers.title: Towards Trustworthy AI: A Review of Ethical and Robust Large Language Models
papers.authors: Md Meftahul Ferdaus, Mahdi Abdelguerfi, Elias Ioup, Kendall N. Niles, Ken Pathak, Steven Sloan
papers.contribution: CSUR survey on ethical and robust LLMs covering unintended harms (including bias/fairness), transparency, adversarial vulnerability, value alignment, and environmental impact. Covers fairness in LLMs. Does NOT cover agentic systems or agent-pipeline components. | DIFF: Ferdaus et al. review trustworthiness of LLMs as text generators across five ethical dimensions; they do not analyse the agent pip

papers.title: The Emerged Security and Privacy of LLM Agent: A Survey with Case Studies
papers.authors: Feng He, Tianqing Zhu, Dayong Ye, Bo Liu, Wanlei Zhou, Philip S. Yu
papers.contribution: CSUR survey specifically on LLM agents, organized around security and privacy threats and defenses across agent components (brain, memory, tool, user, agent, environment). Covers the same six-component agent anatomy we use but through a security/privacy lens. | DIFF: He et al. organize by threat type (privacy leakage, adversarial attacks, jailbreaking) not by fairness. They do not cover demographi

papers.title: A Survey on the Optimization of Large Language Model-based Agents
papers.authors: Shangheng Du, Jiabao Zhao, Jinxin Shi, Zhentao Xie, Xin Jiang, Yanhong Bai, Liang He
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 58, no. 9, Article 223
papers.contribution: CSUR survey on LLM agent optimization covering parameter-driven (fine-tuning, RL) and parameter-free (prompt engineering, retrieval) methods. Agent components covered include planning, memory, tool use. No fairness content. | DIFF: Du et al. optimise for performance (task success, efficiency). We optimise for fairness (demographic parity, counterfactual invariance) — a fundamentally different targ

papers.title: Generalizability of Large Language Model-Based Agents: A Comprehensive Survey
papers.authors: Minxing Zhang, Yi Yang, Roy Xie, Bhuwan Dhingra, Shuyan Zhou, Jian Pei
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 58, no. 10, Article 263
papers.contribution: CSUR survey on LLM agent generalizability — consistent performance across varied instructions, tasks, environments, and domains. Categorizes methods targeting the LLM backbone, agent components, or LLM-component interaction. No fairness content. | DIFF: Zhang et al. define generalizability as performance consistency; we define fairness as outcome parity across demographic groups. These are orthogo

papers.title: LLLMs: A Data-Driven Survey of Evolving Research on Limitations of Large Language Models
papers.authors: Aida Kostikova, Zhipin Wang, Deidamea Bajri, Ole Putz, Benjamin Paassen, Steffen Eger
papers.contribution: CSUR data-driven meta-survey of 14,648 papers on LLM limitations (2022-2025), covering reasoning, generalization, hallucination, bias, and security. Bias/fairness is the fourth most studied limitation category. | DIFF: Kostikova et al. survey LLM limitations at the static-model level with bibliometric methods; their bias category covers QA-era findings. Our survey is scoped exclusively to the agen

papers.title: A Survey on Evaluation of Large Language Models
papers.authors: Yupeng Chang, Xu Wang, Jindong Wang, Yuan Wu, Linyi Yang, Kaijie Zhu, Hao Chen, Xiaoyuan Yi, Cunxiang Wang, Yidong Wang, Wei Ye, Yue Zhang, Yi Chang, Philip S. Yu, Qiang Yang, Xing Xie
papers.venue: ACM Transactions on Intelligent Systems and Technology (TIST), vol. 15, no. 3, Article 39
papers.contribution: Major ACM TIST survey on LLM evaluation covering what to evaluate (knowledge, reasoning, fairness, safety), where to evaluate (datasets, benchmarks), and how to evaluate. Fairness is one evaluation dimension. No agentic evaluation framework. | DIFF: Chang et al. evaluate LLMs as text generators; fairness is one of many capabilities to benchmark. Our §4 constructs an evaluation framework specifical

papers.title: Visibility into AI Agents
papers.authors: Alan Chan, Carson Ezell, Max Kaufmann, Kevin Wei, Lewis Hammond, Herbie Bradley, Emma Bluemke, Nitarshan Rajkumar, David Krueger, Noam Kolt, Lennart Heim, Markus Anderljung
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2024 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT '24)
papers.contribution: FAccT position paper arguing that delegation of complex goals to AI agents exacerbates societal risks. Proposes transparency mechanisms: unique agent tags, real-time monitoring, activity logging. Touches accountability infrastructure relevant to our §8 research agenda. | DIFF: Chan et al. address AI agent visibility and governance mechanisms (what information should be disclosed). We address fairn

papers.title: Software Fairness: An Analysis and Survey
papers.authors: Ezekiel Soremekun, Mike Papadakis, Maxime Cordy, Yves Le Traon
papers.contribution: CSUR survey analysing 164 publications on fairness as a software engineering property, covering specification, testing, and mitigation of bias in learning-based software. Mentions intersectional and sequential fairness as open challenges. | DIFF: Soremekun et al. treat fairness as a software property of ML systems in general, with emphasis on testing and verification methods. They do not cover LLM

papers.title: Preserving the Fairness Guarantees of Classifiers in Changing Environments: A Survey
papers.authors: A. Barrainkua, P. Gordaliza, J. A. Lozano, N. Quadrianto
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 57, no. 6, pp. 1-31
papers.contribution: CSUR survey on fair classification under distribution shift — maintaining fairness when training and test distributions differ. Covers temporal and covariate shift. Tangentially relevant to our §3.6 (long-horizon drift) where deployment feedback loops shift data distributions. | DIFF: Barrainkua et al. address static classifier fairness under distributional shift. We address dynamic agent fairness

papers.title: Triangular Trade-off between Robustness, Accuracy, and Fairness in Deep Neural Networks: A Survey
papers.venue: ACM Computing Surveys, vol. 57, no. 6, Article 140
papers.contribution: CSUR survey on the three-way trade-off between accuracy, robustness, and fairness in deep networks. Relevant as background for our §5 (mitigation) discussion of the safety-utility trade-off in debiasing. | DIFF: Li and Li address the trade-off in static DNN training. Our survey addresses mitigation in acting agents where the trade-off is more complex: component-level interventions can resolve a fa

papers.title: Towards a Better Understanding of Evaluating Trustworthiness in AI Systems
papers.authors: Nils Kemmerzell, Annika Schreiner, Haroon Khalid, Michael Schalk, Letizia Bordoli
papers.contribution: CSUR survey on evaluation strategies for trustworthy AI, deriving an evaluation framework for operationalizing trustworthiness. Fairness is one of the trustworthiness dimensions evaluated. No specific LLM-agent content. | DIFF: Kemmerzell et al. provide a general trustworthiness evaluation framework. Our paper contributes the fairness-specific evaluation infrastructure for LLM-based agents: trajec

papers.title: Trustworthy Information Retrieval in the LLM Era: Bias, Unfairness, and Hallucination
papers.authors: Sunhao Dai, Changle Qu, Sirui Chen, Xiao Zhang, Jun Xu
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2025 Annual International ACM SIGIR Conference (SIGIR-AP '25)
papers.contribution: ACM SIGIR tutorial unifying bias, unfairness, and hallucination in LLM-era IR under a distribution-mismatch framework. Directly relevant to our §3.2 (retrieval bias) and §3.1 (tool selection as an IR problem). | DIFF: Dai et al. frame IR trustworthiness as distribution alignment within a retrieval pipeline. We situate retrieval as one of six agent components and focus on demographic fairness speci

papers.title: Certifying and Removing Disparate Impact
papers.contribution: Canonical formalization of disparate impact in ML; expected in any fairness survey's foundations.

papers.title: Inherent Trade-Offs in the Fair Determination of Risk Scores
papers.contribution: Proves impossibility of jointly satisfying calibration, demographic parity, and equalized odds.

papers.title: Fair Prediction with Disparate Impact: A Study of Bias in Recidivism Prediction Instruments
papers.contribution: Second canonical impossibility result (COMPAS); paired with Kleinberg et al.

papers.title: Fairness and Abstraction in Sociotechnical Systems
papers.contribution: Gold-standard critique of abstraction traps in fair ML; required for sociotechnical scope.

papers.title: Gender Shades: Intersectional Accuracy Disparities in Commercial Gender Classification
papers.contribution: Landmark intersectional accuracy-disparity audit; essential for any intersectionality discussion.

papers.title: Semantics Derived Automatically from Language Corpora Contain Human-Like Biases
papers.contribution: Introduces WEAT; first proof word embeddings encode social biases.

papers.title: Man is to Computer Programmer as Woman is to Homemaker? Debiasing Word Embeddings
papers.contribution: Standard reference for word-embedding debiasing; companion to WEAT.

papers.title: Language (Technology) is Power: A Critical Survey of Bias in NLP
papers.contribution: Foundational critical survey of how bias is conceptualized in NLP.

papers.title: Fairness and Machine Learning: Limitations and Opportunities
papers.contribution: Standard textbook reference for formal fairness definitions.

papers.title: Stereotyping Norwegian Salmon: An Inventory of Pitfalls in Fairness Benchmark Datasets
papers.contribution: Canonical critique of bias benchmarks (StereoSet, CrowS-Pairs, WinoBias).

papers.title: Gender Bias in Coreference Resolution: Evaluation and Debiasing Methods (WinoBias)
papers.contribution: WinoBias dataset; widely used gender-bias coreference benchmark.

papers.title: Gender Bias in Coreference Resolution (WinoGender)
papers.contribution: WinoGender schemas; companion gender-bias benchmark.

papers.title: RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language Models
papers.contribution: Standard toxicity-degeneration benchmark for LMs.

papers.title: ToxiGen: A Large-Scale Machine-Generated Dataset for Adversarial and Implicit Hate Speech Detection
papers.contribution: Large-scale implicit hate-speech/toxicity benchmark.

papers.title: DecodingTrust: A Comprehensive Assessment of Trustworthiness in GPT Models
papers.contribution: NeurIPS Outstanding Paper; comprehensive trustworthiness+fairness assessment of GPT models.

papers.title: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models
papers.contribution: HELM; large-scale multi-metric LM evaluation including fairness.

papers.title: TrustGPT: A Benchmark for Trustworthy and Responsible Large Language Models
papers.contribution: Benchmarks toxicity, bias, and value-alignment across LLMs.

papers.title: Agent AI: Surveying the Horizons of Multimodal Interaction
papers.contribution: Comprehensive multimodal agent survey; expected alongside Xi and Wang surveys.

papers.title: Large Language Model based Multi-Agents: A Survey of Progress and Challenges
papers.contribution: Key multi-agent LLM survey; expected alongside AutoGen/MetaGPT/CAMEL.

papers.title: MetaGPT: Meta Programming for A Multi-Agent Collaborative Framework
papers.contribution: Canonical multi-agent software-team system; relevant to task-allocation bias.

papers.title: CAMEL: Communicative Agents for Mind Exploration of Large Language Model Society
papers.contribution: First role-playing multi-agent framework; relevant to persona/delegation bias.

papers.title: ChatDev: Communicative Agents for Software Development
papers.contribution: Multi-agent software-development pipeline; relevant to delegation/task allocation.

papers.title: HuggingGPT: Solving AI Tasks with ChatGPT and its Friends in Hugging Face
papers.contribution: Canonical tool-orchestration agent; relevant to tool-selection.

papers.title: WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents
papers.contribution: Standard web-agent benchmark; relevant to trajectory-level evaluation gap.

papers.title: Mind2Web: Towards a Generalist Agent for the Web
papers.contribution: Companion generalist web-agent benchmark.

papers.title: SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?
papers.contribution: Standard coding-agent benchmark; relevant to agentic task evaluation.

papers.title: Tool Learning with Large Language Models: A Survey
papers.contribution: Comprehensive tool-learning survey; expected in tool-selection section.

papers.title: AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents
papers.contribution: Multi-environment LLM-agent benchmark; relevant to evaluation discussion.

papers.title: Towards Understanding Sycophancy in Language Models
papers.contribution: Definitive sycophancy study; sycophancy is a long-horizon fairness failure.

papers.title: Whose Opinions Do Language Models Reflect?
papers.contribution: Shows LMs misrepresent demographic group opinions; relevant to personalization fairness.

papers.title: Toxicity in ChatGPT: Analyzing Persona-assigned Language Models
papers.contribution: Persona assignment increases toxicity up to 6x; foundational for delegation/persona bias.

papers.title: Kelly is a Warm Person, Joseph is a Role Model: Gender Biases in LLM-Generated Reference Letters
papers.contribution: LLM-generated professional documents perpetuate gender stereotypes.

papers.title: White Men Lead, Black Women Help? Benchmarking and Mitigating Language Agency Social Biases in LLMs
papers.contribution: LABE benchmark; intersectional language-agency bias in LLM-generated texts.

papers.title: The Woman Worked as a Babysitter: On Biases in Language Generation
papers.contribution: Introduces the regard metric for NLG bias.

papers.title: Dissecting Racial Bias in an Algorithm Used to Manage the Health of Populations
papers.contribution: Landmark real-world healthcare algorithmic-bias audit.

papers.title: Mitigating Bias in Algorithmic Hiring: Evaluating Claims and Practices
papers.contribution: Standard reference for algorithmic hiring bias claims and practices.

papers.title: Machine Bias (COMPAS investigation)
papers.contribution: Investigation that triggered the COMPAS fairness debate; widely cited.

papers.title: TrustLLM: Trustworthiness in Large Language Models
papers.contribution: Comprehensive trustworthiness benchmark (incl. fairness) across 16 LLMs.

papers.title: Artificial Intelligence Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)
papers.contribution: Standard US AI governance/risk framework; expected for governance anchoring.

papers.title: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (Artificial Intelligence Act)
papers.contribution: First comprehensive AI regulation; regulates high-stakes bias domains.

papers.title: Concrete Problems in AI Safety
papers.contribution: Foundational AI-safety framing; reward hacking/side effects are fairness-adjacent.

papers.title: Language Models Don't Always Say What They Think: Unfaithful Explanations in Chain-of-Thought Prompting
papers.contribution: CoT can reinforce social stereotypes without stating them; key for planning-bias section.

papers.title: Can Large Language Models Reason and Plan?
papers.venue: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences
papers.contribution: Foundational critique of LLM planning capability; relevant to planning-decomposition.

papers.title: Biases in Large Language Models: Origins, Inventory, and Discussion
papers.contribution: ACM-published taxonomy of LLM bias types; CSUR reviewers note its omission.

papers.title: Persistent Anti-Muslim Bias in Large Language Models
papers.contribution: Shows religion-based bias persists after fine-tuning; relevant to multi-group fairness.

papers.title: Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses: The PRISMA Statement
papers.contribution: The PRISMA statement; standard protocol for systematic review reporting.
